_comment: SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for all 5 landing-page versions. Facts/copy live here once; each bespoke page draws from this. Update numbers HERE only.

product.name: Evolve Loop
product.oneLiner: Any agent can write code overnight. Evolve Loop is the layer that decides whether that code is safe to merge — adversarially, structurally, and with memory that compounds across runs.
product.oneBreath: Your AI wrote the code. A different AI tried to break it. A bash exit code — not an opinion — decided whether it ships.

hero.headline: Ship the code your agent wrote — without reading the diff.
hero.headlineLead: Ship the code your agent wrote —
headlineVariants: Ship the code your agent wrote — without reading the diff.
red_count == 0 → ship. Everything else is an opinion.
Merge while you sleep. The lessons survive the crash.
An auditor that can't be fooled. A ledger that can't be forged.
hero.subhead: Evolve Loop runs every AI change through an adversarial pipeline: a different model family tries to refute it, a deterministic predicate suite gates the merge, and every failure becomes a lesson the next cycle reads.

problem.heading: Agents can write the feature now. The hard part is merging it.
body: Autonomous agents are good enough to write a feature, fix a bug, or refactor a module unattended. The question is no longer can the agent write the code? — it's can you merge what it wrote without re-reading every diff?

The industry's default answer is "ask another LLM if it looks done." That judge is the weakest link: a single model grading code — often a sibling of the one that wrote it — shares the same blind spots, prefers its own style, and rewards confident prose over correctness.

theTurn.heading: LLMs do the judgment. Deterministic code owns the verdict.
body: Scout decides what to build, Builder decides how, Auditor decides what looks wrong — only a model does these well. But phase ordering, write-path scoping, ship gating, the verdict, ledger hashing — all live in the Go kernel, where the model has no vote.
A model can be wrong, biased, or adversarial. It cannot reorder phases, write outside its worktree, ship without a green verdict, or forge the ledger. Those aren't prompts — they're code.

phaseSpine.blurb: Turns a vague goal into 8 structured fields + at least one challenged premise.
Finds the real work, cites research, reads prior lessons before choosing.
Bounds the cycle — top_n / deferred / dropped — so scope can't blob.
Writes the code + predicates inside an isolated git worktree.
A different model family cross-checks; the predicate suite runs; the verdict is a binary PASS/FAIL.
Commits to main only if red_count == 0. Tamper-evident, SHA-chained.
Every failure becomes a durable lesson the next cycle's Scout reads.

pillars.title: Verdicts are bash exit codes, not LLM prose
pillars.claim: The ship gate reads a number, not a vibe.
pillars.proof: EGPS predicates are executable scripts; their exit code is the verdict. The model can write prose all day — only acs-verdict.json:red_count == 0 ships. A failed test cannot be hallucinated.

pillars.title: Three layers of structural anti-gaming
pillars.claim: It's hard to fool because control is in code, not judgment.
pillars.proof: Tier 1: PreToolUse hooks + a SHA-chained ledger (tamper any past entry and every later link breaks). Tier 2: OS sandbox + a per-cycle git worktree. Tier 3: adversarial defaults. Tier 1 is non-negotiable.

pillars.title: Adversarial by construction
pillars.claim: The reviewer is built to refute, not rubber-stamp.
pillars.proof: The Auditor is a different model family from the Builder (e.g. Opus auditing Sonnet), prompted to break the work. A TDD-engineer writes the failing predicates before the Builder writes code — author and verifier are never the same.

pillars.title: Memory that compounds across runs
pillars.claim: The loop gets smarter; you don't repeat yourself.
pillars.proof: Failures produce YAML lesson files merged into state. 61 durable lessons now fire across 367 cycles — some authored 90 cycles ago still catch bugs today. This is the Reflexion loop, wired through evidence-bound storage.

pillars.title: Vendor-agnostic, per-phase routing
pillars.claim: The best model for each role — no lock-in.
pillars.proof: A native Go resolver routes each phase to a CLI/model independently: Scout on one model, Builder on another, Auditor on a third. Claude, Gemini, and Codex adapters ship; the orchestrator doesn't care which ran.

incident.heading: The framework caught its own bugs.
incident.body: A Gemini-3.1-pro experiment shipped with damage — 7 distinct bugs: a Builder that skipped Scout's deliverable, an Auditor that cited out-of-scope files, a mis-set capability flag. None reached you silently. The predicate suite, ledger forensics, and retrospective surfaced all seven; within 48 hours each became a structural guard in v10.7.0. The value isn't "cycles never fail" — it's "failures produce durable fixes."

comparison.heading: When to reach for it.
rows: Different model family, prompted to refute
comparison.note: Devin, OpenHands and Aider are agents. Evolve Loop is the governance pipeline that can drive them and add verification + learning on top. We don't compete on how fast the agent codes — on whether you can merge what it wrote without reading the diff.

quickstart.heading: Your first cycle is one command away.

finalCta.heading: Stop reading diffs you didn't write.
finalCta.subhead: Open source, MIT-licensed, runs on Claude Code, Gemini CLI, and Codex CLI.

footer.grounding: Grounded in Reflexion (Shinn et al. 2023) and double-loop learning (Argyris & Schön 1978).
